sentences.text: Something I forgot to mention is about a man by the name of [?
Capita ?] [?
Mugello. ?] He was almost the head man from the Czechoslovak army.
He came over there.
According my knowledge, he [? met ?] his wife, because his wife was Jewish.
To Treblinka.
He was working there.
He was in the same barrack with me.
He was one of the head men to organize the uprising in Treblinka, which actually, the uprising was on August 2nd 1943, which I wasn't there already.
I escaped in January of 1943.
But after the war I find out that he was one of the leader.
It is my responsibility to mention his name at this time.
Yes.
Yes, I know him.
I spoke to him many times at night, when we were together in the barrack.
About the situation.
The way it is.
What to do.
Yes.
Yes, he was together-- he was together with [?
Horajinski. ?] He was together with [?
Gilleski. ?] He was together with [?
Mitrakovsky. ?] He was also together with the [?
Masarik, ?] which they were very close friend, and a few more of that.
When it happened, we had an organization, an underground organization, in Czestochowa.
There were some leaders of them.
One of them, I would like to mention a woman by the name of Rifka Glanz.
There was a few men.
A very close friend of mine, we were together in the concentration camp-- I mean, before the war even.
He was a leader of the Zionist organization, by the name of Nathan Schlamnitsky, with his brother.
There was from various organization, like in the ghetto was organized one party which has to organize in organizing the uprising, whatever we could.
And that was the organization from one communist Zionist religious organization.
We had one organization that is the Jewish Underground, by that name.
Yeah, Jewish Underground.
We had some weapons.
But the story with weapons is very tragic, because for no money we could get it.
And this we could buy, only by the Pollocks.
And the Pollocks.
Were not interested of-- of our survival.
We had some.
Just guns.
And then we started to make our own Molotov bottles, if you know what that is.
The Molotov-- the Molotov you fill in with-- how you call?
Oil.
Or you fill in with some kind of gas.
And by throwing them, they explode.
Well, it happened also that by a selection the one which went from Czestochowa, one of our men from Treblinka by the name of Fislewicz, had a gun.
And when they selected people, a number of people, say a few hundred of it, he got out from his group.
Like there were groups, 25, 30 in a group.
And he opened fire on a German.
But the gun was so good they couldn't kill them.
At that time, they took out 27 from the boys, from our boys.
The finest.
And in front of us, they killed them.
After that, we went in back to the ghetto where we worked about another couple months.
And we had a man which was really one of the finest personal friend of mine.
One of them was Gamolinski, and the other one I don't recall the name.
How can I forget the name like that?
Will to come to it.
They caught him, and they find out he is the organizer.
They ripped him apart.
But they couldn't get out a word of him.
It was a day before that, on that 26 of June, 1943, they liquidated the small ghetto.
They bombed all the houses in the small ghetto, which was left, and a lot of people perished at the bunkers.
Hiding.
With some kids, too.
And the ghetto did not exist anymore.
A number of the people were killed.
And the big majority went in to the concentration camp, the labor camp, by the name of HASAG.
HASAG.
That was in Czestochowa.
HASAG is Hugo Schneider Aktiengesellschaft.
That's where they first let us come from, that one of the big factories from ammunition in Germany.
Yes.
Yeah, I went over there too.
And we worked.
Condition was very bad.
It was a-- it was a labor camp.
Mostly--
For men and men.
Men and women, yes.
Mostly people worked ammunition, [? they ?] [? worked ?] [? out. ?] I myself, I didn't work in the factory.
I worked in the camp as a barber.
Cutting-- cutting hair.
Men.
Men.
Prisoners only.
Nobody else could come in over there.
So the-- who guarded the camp?
The German [NON-ENGLISH].
Not me.
There was some barbers.
I never cut the German hair.
There was some barbers--
Yes, it was.
Barracks.
We were in the one barrack, about a couple hundred people.
Laying down, it was like one lay, second, then a third one.
Each body to-- body to body.
Yeah, I was.
I was most of the friends, yes.
No, I was--
I-- I just a barber.
I had no assignment.
Not exactly, but to go by.
To go by with.
Yeah.
It will-- kommandant was one German by the name of Dr.
Litt.
Litt.
But if he would be alive, I myself, and hundred of people more will testify not against him, but for him.
Because he was a nice, kind man.
Where we starved to get bread, he got the bread for us for a very small price.
And he did a lot for us.
There's no one from that camp which would say a bad word against him.
Nothing else.
He couldn't do nothing more, because on top of him, he had the gestapo.
But generally, the camp in Czestochowa considered to be one of the best camp in existence of the German concentration camps.
Nothing.
Nothing specially.
Nothing.
I was-- I was assigned to work in a hospital by people who had typhus.
And I worked with them.
And beside this, I worked also in the barbershop.
Established a barber shop exactly like a barber shop from outside.
And worked there.
Yes, old tools.
[INAUDIBLE] We had always.
Nobody took away from us.
And we did that-- that job.
No, I wouldn't say I did.
I know that people got killed, shot.
I know they almost killed a woman also, she got shot in their eye.
But I wouldn't say that I was to see it, because they were outside in the factory, and I was in the place assigned to-- for the barbershop.
Nothing that I know of.
I tell you why.
There were no place where to escape.
If you say escape, you got to go somewhere.
To be-- to be outside between the Pollocks was even worse than to be by the Germans.
So in other word, the escaping didn't mean a thing.
To escape, you got to have a place where to be hidden.
If you have no place like that, forget about it.
Always.
I don't know.
In my mind, I always had.
Even in the toughest time, I had.
I had always in my mind that I'm going to survive.
I don't know why, but that was.
And we-- we were there I would say a long time until January 16, 1945.
But ?] we were liberated.
By the Russian.
But before the liberation, I escaped from there.
Because it was the night before the liberation.
And they supposed to get rid of all the people there by exploding the factory with everything.
And myself and more people saw they're carrying the bombs for it.
But they had no time to do it.
The Russians were on top of them.
Around 10 o'clock at night, I went, at that time.
I went with a girl, my wife.
Yes.
I met her in the ghetto.
The small ghetto.
When I got back, she came over.
And she asked me about her husband.
She was married before that.
It happened that I find out that her husband was some kind of cousin of mine.
And he was at Treblinka.
He worked.
And I told her the story, how it is.
After he worked for a while over there, he got killed.
And we got, you know, together.
Like coming.
And it wasn't so-- so simple, even in the ghetto.
Which I'm far off the ghetto.
There-- in the ghetto were three streets.
One street was for men.
The second street was for married couple.
And the third street were for ladies, unmarried ladies.
In another word is if somebody-- like it happened to a relative of mine who lived in the street where the men lived.
He wanted to see his sister which is on the street of the girls.
They killed him, because it was not allowed.
There was only three streets over there.
It happened she was working-- I was in a barbershop also in the-- in the ghetto.
In the small ghetto I worked in a barbershop.
She should come-- she could come in, doing a cleaning or other things.
So we met and we got together.
It's already over 50 years.
Yes.
There was a rabbi over there.
My wife's name is Regina Hamburger.
She is from Lodz, Poland.
It was right after I got back from traveling.
Yeah, we were working.
We work in HASAG.
We could see each other in the HASAG, every day.
Yes.
Yes, when I planned this, because I didn't want-- I told her.
Listen to me.
I will never go into a [INAUDIBLE] again.
Even if I get killed, I don't go in.
If I escape, OK.
If not, let them kill me.
But don't go with-- don't go after me, because there is no such thing like they're taking men and woman together.
Well, it happened that before I left the barbed wire, and there was a big stop already between the Ukrainian and the Germans.
I said, hey, come.
We go together.
And the following morning, we were liberated by the Russian army.
How we know is [? were ?] the Russian.
Well, they said they're going-- they're going to Berlin to liberate [INAUDIBLE].
There was, you know, some kind of joy.
But some kind of thing that-- first thing, we were left alone without nobody, without family, without clothes, without money, without anything.
And the first few days, it was very hard even with food.
We had nothing to eat.
In the concentration camp, at least we had a piece of bread, a soup, little water, something.
But outside, we didn't have nobody.
They didn't have themselves.
They slept in-- in-- in February-- January, February.
They slept in street.
And they didn't go in to take some apartments or tell people to go out [? of them. ?]
After the liberation, I was my home town.
But I couldn't stand it.
Every place, every little street I went by, it was some kind of mix up in my mind, because I saw people [? was ?] who were relatives, which in my mind.
Which actually, they were dead.
I left the place.
I got in touch with a few more barbers, and went back to that place I come from, Beuthen.
And we took over a barbershop and a beauty shop from a German woman, which she was willing, very, very willing to give it to us, and not to give to Poles or to give to Russian.
And she worked over there with us.
And that happened from February until up to June.
And then, in Poland, it was created an army.
Army [NON-ENGLISH] army, they call themselves.
And what they did is to kill Jewish people after the liberation, which a lot of Jewish people got killed.
Over 3,000 in Poland. 3,2000 Jewish people got killed after the liberation.
It happened across the street where I-- I had the shop.
Two of them, officers, came in to a jewelry store without demanding anything, without taking anything.
They just took out their guns and they killed them.
I said, that time, to my wife, we have to leave.
Same night, makes ready, and went to the German border.
We maked our papers over there and went to Germany.
Oh, Germany, we went to a place called before Baumberg.
It was-- is in Germany.
And from there we went to a little place by the name of Falkenstein, which is near Innsbruck.
Famous city.
No.
No, just at the end, before going to United State.
I was a few months in a place by the name of Foehrenwald.
Oh, I would say about four or five months.
No.
According to camp, it wasn't too bad.
We had the UNRRA, which you know, they supplied us with food, packages, clothing, and for the babies.
And we had-- there was also some kind of organization, the-- some Zionist organization which had tried to bring people to-- illegal to Israel.
It was just like life in a small Jewish shtetl.
No.
Yeah.
I came to America '51.
No, no, came by ship.
Ship General Eltinge.
To New York.
Oh, we got-- we had-- my wife had-- had a brother, [? but ?] he himself wasn't settled yet.
So we went into [? a house. ?] So my wife stayed in a very big room with another 600, 700 women.
And I was also at that kind of-- amount of men together.
Until we find ourselves something.
We have a daughter.
Bonnie.
Bonnie [?
Tobie ?] after my mother.
She is in Florida now.
She has two daughters and a small child.
Barber.
Oh, I tried, but I-- I couldn't do it.
Because first thing, they didn't believe me.
The second thing, they said that there's something wrong with-- not only with-- not with me, with some other people the same thing.
And that what we were in a concentration camp there was a reason that the German took us to the concentration camp.
It was very hard for us.
It was very hard to get an apartment.
It was very hard to get a job also.
I started working $15 a week.
But we managed.
I thank-- thank god we didn't need nothing from nobody.
We didn't get anything from nobody.
We weren't-- we wasn't supported, not by a jury, not by the Jewish Federation.
Yes.
Yes.
I was called to be a witness of the one that was the first one-- it was, if I'm not mistaken, 1951 of a trial from a man from the Treblinka extermination camp by the name of Josef Hirtreiter.
That trial was in Frankfurt.
Oh, I testified to that time, first thing, in Germany was a little simple like it is here in the United States.
In the United States, they bring the men in front.
He sits next to the judge or what.
Over there, no.
Over there, when I came in, we were about five, six witnesses.
Over-- I just-- I just say what I-- when it-- what happened to me.
When I came into trial, the judge ask your name.
So and so.
You are a witness for this and this?
Yes.
Will you recognize the man?
I said, I don't know.
It's quite a few years.
Maybe.
No, he said, do you see the man here?
The man sit in middle of the public.
There's 500, 600 people.
He wears civil clothes like anybody else.
But the eyes you can never forget.
And I pointed out at him.
So [? they ?] callled him in on the bench.
And at that time I-- I testified.
Another time I was over this in Dusseldorf against 10 or 11 from Treblinka.
One of them in court, [?
Franz ?] [NON-ENGLISH] was [NON-ENGLISH], was [NON-ENGLISH], was-- I don't remember all the name of that.
At that time, the same thing we testified.
And they were find guilty.
Some-- some of them, they got life.
Three of them got life.
The other got 15 years, 10 years, 6 years.
One got free.
Oh, I don't know the name.
But I-- I was there when he came out of there.
Seeing the people on trial, that is-- that's a difficult.
You see the men which went around all day long with a gun, killing people.
And he says he is not guilty, he didn't do anything.
He just did what the Fuhrer told him to do.
He had an order.
Just according to order, they went.
I just saw him outside going around.
But I never had any word with him.
I never--
--spoke him.
No, no.
No, no.
He wasn't at the trial.
I don't know.
No, I never-- I never know what had happened to him.
Just the-- just the two trials attended, yes.
That's more than enough for me.
Believe me, when you get back from a trial like that, you are more sick than alive.
It's not a pleasure.
Even coming here, believe me, it's not a pleasure.
Inside, even if you say with a smile all this, that, inside you can never forget.
You will never forget what a turmoil you went through.
